skill.name: github-actions-templates
skill.description: Create GitHub Actions workflow templates for testing, Docker builds, Kubernetes deployment, matrix builds, security scans, and approval-based releases.
skill.summary: Generate practical GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow templates for common build, test, scan, and deployment needs.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution and credential findings are mostly false positives from Markdown GitHub Actions examples. A real medium-risk issue remains because two third-party Actions are pinned to master, which can change without review.

medium_findings.title: Unpinned Third-Party GitHub Actions
medium_findings.description: The security scan examples use aquasecurity/trivy-action@master and snyk/actions/node@master. Branch references can change without review and may introduce supply-chain risk in generated workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly reference third-party Actions through the mutable master branch. This is a known CI supply-chain risk, although it appears to be an insecure template choice rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections occur in Markdown code fences, resource references, or related skill names. I found no evidence that the skill itself executes Ruby, shell commands, or user-controlled input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation or YAML workflow snippets inside SKILL.md. They are not executable code in the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: Workflow Secret Placeholders Are Documented Examples
low_findings.description: The GitHub and AWS token references use GitHub Actions secrets syntax in workflow templates. This is expected for CI/CD examples, but users must manage these secrets securely.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential references are placeholders under GitHub Actions with: blocks. I found no evidence that the skill reads or exfiltrates local environment variables.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Production URL Is Not Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is a deployment environment example using https://app.example.com. I found no evidence of data transfer to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domain is the reserved example.com domain and appears inside a GitHub Actions environment field. It is documentation, not active network code.

low_findings.title: Hidden GitHub Workflow Path Is Expected
low_findings.description: The hidden path detection points to ./.github/workflows/reusable-test.yml. This is the standard location for reusable GitHub Actions workflows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path is a conventional repository workflow path. I found no evidence of hidden file harvesting or unauthorized filesystem access.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match ordinary words in the skill description, kubectl describe command, and related skill name. I found no cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose or Kubernetes commands, not hash, cipher, or signing logic. The static signal is a lexical false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mutable Action Version References
dangerous_patterns.description: Two workflow examples use @master for third-party Actions. Templates should prefer pinned version tags or commit SHAs for reproducible CI behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The mutable references are visible in the workflow examples. The risk is supply-chain drift, not confirmed malicious code.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose time building reliable GitHub Actions workflows from scratch. This skill provides practical templates for tests, builds, scans, deployments, and reusable workflows.
actual_capabilities: Creates GitHub Actions examples for Node.js test workflows with dependency caching.
Provides Docker image build and push workflow patterns for GitHub Container Registry.
Shows Kubernetes deployment workflows using AWS credentials and kubectl commands.
Defines matrix build examples across operating systems and Python versions.
Includes reusable workflow, security scan, and production approval patterns.
limitations: Templates must be adapted to each repository, package manager, and deployment target.
The skill does not validate generated workflows against a live GitHub repository.
Users must provide and protect required secrets in GitHub Actions settings.
Third-party Action versions should be reviewed before production use.

use_cases.title: Set up CI for a new repository
use_cases.description: Create a test workflow that installs dependencies, runs lint checks, executes tests, and uploads coverage.

use_cases.title: Automate container releases
use_cases.description: Build and publish Docker images to a registry when branches or release tags are pushed.

use_cases.title: Standardize deployment workflows
use_cases.description: Create reusable workflows for deployments, security scans, approval gates, and repeated pipeline steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic test workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a repository without CI.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow for a Node.js project that installs dependencies, runs lint, runs tests, and caches npm packages.

prompt_templates.title: Add Docker image publishing
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that builds a Docker image, tags it from branch and release data, and publishes it to GitHub Container Registry.

prompt_templates.title: Design a deployment pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that deploys to Kubernetes after a release tag, uses environment approvals, and verifies the rollout.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor into reusable workflows
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert repeated GitHub Actions test and deployment steps into reusable workflows with typed inputs, required secrets, and clear caller examples.

output_examples.input: Request a CI workflow for a Node.js service.
output_examples.output: A workflow plan with checkout, Node setup, dependency caching, install, lint, test, and coverage upload steps.
A build pipeline outline that logs in to a registry, creates image metadata, builds the image, and publishes tags.
A deployment workflow description with protected environments, cloud credentials, Kubernetes apply commands, and rollout verification.

best_practices: Pin third-party Actions to reviewed versions or commit SHAs before production use.
Use least-privilege GitHub token permissions and environment protection rules.
Keep secrets in GitHub Actions secrets or environment secrets, never in workflow files.
anti_patterns: Do not copy templates without adapting branch names, paths, secrets, and deployment targets.
Do not give workflows broad write permissions when read-only access is enough.
Do not use mutable third-party Action branches for sensitive production workflows.

faq.question: Can this skill create workflows for any project?
faq.answer: It provides common templates, but each workflow needs project-specific commands, paths, secrets, and deployment settings.

faq.question: Does it run GitHub Actions for me?
faq.answer: No. It helps create workflow content and guidance. GitHub runs the workflows after they are added to a repository.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it handle secrets securely?
faq.answer: It shows GitHub Actions secrets syntax. Users remain responsible for creating, rotating, and limiting access to secrets.

faq.question: Can it create deployment workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for Docker publishing, Kubernetes deployment, production approvals, and rollout verification.

faq.question: What should be reviewed before publishing workflows?
faq.answer: Review third-party Action versions, token permissions, secret names, runner choices, and deployment commands.
